title: The Eleventh Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver | August 4, 2024

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Eleventh Sunday after Pentecost falls within Ordinary Time in the Christian calendar. Under the Revised Common Lectionary (RCL), the readings would likely include a passage from 2 Samuel (probably 11:26-12:13a, the account of David and Bathsheba), Psalm 51, a selection from Ephesians (likely 4:1-16 on Christian unity and spiritual gifts), and John 6:24-35 (Jesus as the Bread of Life, part of the extended Bread of Life discourse). The season emphasizes continued spiritual growth after Pentecost, with focus on discipleship and living out Christian ethics in ordinary circumstances.

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

In summer 2024, the Episcopal Church was in the post-General Convention period following the 79th General Convention held in Louisville in June 2024, which addressed ongoing issues including LGBTQ+ inclusion, sexuality/marriage canons, and climate action. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Cheryl Pero (elected 2022), would have been engaged in implementing those resolutions at the diocesan level. No specific major episcopal scandals or leadership changes were in the immediate news cycle during early August 2024.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Colorado was experiencing typical late summer weather patterns with some wildfire activity across the state, though not at crisis levels in early August. Denver's downtown and Capitol Hill neighborhood were focused on late summer tourism and events; the Democratic National Convention was set to occur in Chicago the following week (August 19-22), drawing national political attention away from Colorado. Local concerns included housing affordability and homelessness, perennial issues affecting the Cathedral's urban location.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The nation was in the final stretch before the August Democratic National Convention, with Kamala Harris having officially secured the presidential nomination following Joe Biden's withdrawal from the race on July 21. Vice President Harris had selected Minnesota Governor Tim Walz as her running mate on August 6 (days after this sermon), energizing Democratic voters. The 2024 presidential race was in full swing with the Republican ticket (Trump-Vance) dominating conservative media. Gun violence, healthcare, and reproductive rights (post-Dobbs decision) remained central political issues. The summer featured ongoing coverage of the Israel-Hamas conflict aftermath and international tensions.

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Israel-Gaza conflict remained a major international story, though media attention in the US was partly eclipsed by domestic political events. Ukraine continued its war with Russia, now in its third year, with ongoing casualties and diplomatic stalemate. The Paris Olympics had recently concluded (August 4 was the closing ceremony day), which had dominated international sports coverage for the previous two weeks and may have been fresh in the congregation's minds.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The cultural moment was defined by political polarization and fatigue, with the 2024 presidential election dominating conversation and media landscape. There was a sense of transition and cautious optimism among Democrats following Harris's nomination. Church attendance across mainline denominations reflected broader American religious decline, though Episcopal churches maintained strong communities around social justice issues and inclusive theology. The Paris Olympics closing ceremony on August 4 provided a moment of international unity and cultural spectacle, contrasting sharply with domestic political divisions.
